original_abstract:
Founded in 1863, the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC) has long been revered as the gold standard of humanitarianism. However, despite a century and a half of significant service, a growing chorus of critics—including former officials—contends that the organization is currently in decline. These observers argue that the ICRC has compromised its core principles of neutrality and independence, while an aggressive expansion in size and budget has eroded its reputation for being a lean, agile, and effective force in the field. 

Drawing on the organization's historical record and recent internal shifts, David P. Forsythe evaluates these claims, specifically examining policy decisions that have blurred the lines between humanitarian action and Swiss national interests. While acknowledging that the ICRC has made strategic errors and faced moral crises—most notably during the 1930s and 40s—Forsythe argues that the organization is not in a fatal downward spiral. Instead, he suggests that while certain corrections are necessary to restore its "moral compass," reports of the ICRC's demise are premature. This analysis reframes the current crisis not as an end, but as a critical juncture requiring a return to principled, independent action.
humanized_abstract:
International Committee of the Red Cross 1863 It’s widely regarded as the embodiment of humanitarianism. Yet 150 years after its creation, the ICRC is being attacked by increasing numbers of people including former members of its staff. To these critics, the Geneva-based body has lost its way. It has, they say, abandoned many of the impartial and neutral principles that have defined its work. At the same time, the ICRC’s dramatic expansion in recent years – in terms of staff numbers and budget – has made it a lumbering giant that has lost its former efficiency and frugality.

Grounded in historical documentation and drawing on recent events within the organization, in this paper David P. Forsythe assesses these competing claims, and examines in some detail the humanitarian ICRC’s recent decisions to blend what may appear to many as distinctly humanitarian and purely national interests. Although the organization has clearly committed its share of what can be regarded as strategic errors and faced the kinds of moral dilemma that threaten an organisation’s dignity — as occurred with particularly disturbing results in the 1930s and 1940s — this author does not think that the organisation is in a process of fatal decay. Although apparently required to restore ICRC’s ‘moral compass’ in some areas, the notion of the humanitarian organisation’s irrelevance and imminent collapse appears to be premature. Rather than the ICRC being at an end-point of crisis, the humanitarian organisation is arguably at a turning-point.
